channel.title: PINtPRESS
channel.description: Long-form writing from the PINtPOINT team on beer, pubs, and the tools we build. Postcards from beer cities, guides to crawls and styles, and notes on what's new in the app.

item.title: It's Not Just the Sunshine: Your Holiday Beer Really Is a Different Beer
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: The Estrella you drank in Barcelona is not the Estrella they pour in Manchester. Damm bought the Bedford Eagle Brewery outright from Carlsberg Marston's in 2023 to brew a 4.6% UK version of a 5.4% Spanish original. Madri Excepcional has never seen Spain. What CAMRA's July 2026 Beer in the UK Report exposes about the lagers on every British bar — and why calibrating your PINtDEXTER against a holiday beer isn't the same as calibrating against the version poured in your local.

item.title: The Beer Final We Almost Had: England vs Spain · The Parallel-Timeline Pub VAR Verdict
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Argentina beat England 2-1 in the semi-final on Wednesday. This piece was drafted for the beer final we might have had — a Berlin rematch of English cask against Spain's Iberian craft column. Publishing it anyway as the parallel-timeline read. Draught Bass hat-trick, a 101st-minute ghost goal, Estrella Damm and La Pirata Black Block. The Pub VAR booth stands by its verdict. The actual final (Argentina vs Spain) follows in a separate piece.

item.title: Norman Balon: London's Last Great Rude Landlord
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: The Coach and Horses landlord who ran the Greek Street pub for sixty-three years and made a public brand of his own rudeness has died aged 99. On the fortnightly Private Eye lunch, the Waterhouse play, the 2005 licensing regulations he wouldn't sit down and read — and why a room isn't the same room once the person who kept it wrong on purpose has gone.

item.title: Humphrey Smith: British Beer's Great Awkward Character
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: The Samuel Smith's beer baron who ran Britain's strangest pub chain by his own private rule has died aged 81. On cheap pints, dark wood, shire horses, boarded-up villages and a row over an air freshener — and on why the whole thing may become easier without him and also less peculiar.

item.title: Before Kick-Off: Why English Cask Has the Advantage
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: The football is heading to the business end. The Beer World Cup is heading the same way. With the Perfect Pint Promise just launched and heritage cask back on the rise — Greene King selling Old Speckled Hen to Spanish lager giant Damm, Draught Bass tripling distribution this year, JW Lees relaunching cask Boddingtons — the case for English cask is loud and current. The squad of eleven hand-pulled pints we'd send into a Beer World Cup, the moat argument that says cask is the one format the world can't quite copy, and a forward-look to Cask Ale Week 17–27 September.

item.title: World Cup 2026 Stadium Beer Map: Help Us Find Good Beer Near The Grounds
item.dc:creator: PINtPOINT
item.description: A first-pass map of bars, pubs, breweries, taprooms, bottle shops and beer gardens around all 16 World Cup 2026 host stadiums across the USA, Canada and Mexico. Atlanta, Seattle, Philadelphia, Vancouver and Toronto landed cleanly; Dallas, the Bay Area and Mexico City need local knowledge; MetLife is still the loudest help-wanted flag. First pass, not the final whistle. Open PINtPOINT near a host stadium and add what we missed.

item.title: Beer World Cup XI: 48 Nations, Real Fixtures, Stupidly Serious Beer Results
item.dc:creator: PINtPOINT
item.description: The football teams play on the pitch. The beers play in the pub. Every country in the World Cup gets a beer squad; real fixtures drive the matchups; Pub VAR, The Selection Committee and The Pint Desk arbitrate the disputes. A serialised editorial tournament loosely based on a distant memory of Loaded magazine's lost Beer World Cup. Help us pick the squads.

item.title: Beerology — The Anatomy of a Pint
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: What does this beer actually feel like to drink? Eight axes — Body, Bitterness, Sweetness, Roast, Fruit, Finish, Softness, Complexity — plus four drinker cues, a drinkability bucket and one plain-English gut-take sentence per beer. Built for the 800-millisecond tap-list decision; honest about the sour and Belgian-yeast styles it doesn't cover. Capstone of the four-essay arc on choosing the right pint.

item.title: Following the Firkin trail(s)…
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: In 1979 David Bruce opened the Goose & Firkin in Elephant and Castle. By 1995 the chain had 44 pubs, 19 of them brewing on site. On 8 October 1999 the brewing stopped — and that, not the 2001 brand-discontinuation, is when Firkin actually died. By 2001 the brand was gone too, but the buildings (mostly still pubs under other names) outlived it. The 12 original London Firkin sites traced — deliberately — the outline of a dog's head on a 1990 paper leaflet. Companion: walk the Firkin Ale Trail Hall-of-Fame crawl in PINtPOINT.

item.title: Anchor Brewing's British heritage runs deeper than people realise
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Three of Anchor's signature beers were lifted from English brewing on a 1975 Yorkshire pub tour. The Great American Beer Festival was modelled on CAMRA's GBBF. For fifteen years a Halifax distributor kept Anchor on UK draught. The companion to Friday's piece on Anchor's revival: the case that Anchor's identity was always partly English — and what that means for a return to UK taps.

item.title: Anchor Brewing looks to be coming back. The 2021 rebrand isn't.
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Construction crews at Potrero Hill, twenty months of silence from a billionaire owner, and a homepage in May 2026 still wearing the 1896 logo — not the 2021 mark drinkers hated. Anchor's revival, as the SF Chronicle and others now report, is moving from rumour into pre-brewing infrastructure. What's coming back, on the evidence, is the older Anchor — even if Headlands Brewing's Austin Sharp expects "properly operational for 2027" rather than this year.

item.title: Under the Hood: How PINtDEXTER Picks Your Pint
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: The engineering follow-up to PINtPOINT's beer-recommender essay. The four layers PINtDEXTER stacks under the hood — preference capture, manual sliders, per-beer LLM-inferred flavour DNA, and venue-aware scoring — plus the misclassified pale lager that exposed the most recent fix. References yhat's 2013 R prototype and Kushwaha's 2023 LLM-on-comments work to trace the 2013 to 2026 arc of beer recommendation.

item.title: Save Our Pubs, In One Pub: The Bell at Ingatestone
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: We walked into the Bell at Ingatestone on Saturday to claim a free pint of Telegraph Ale. PINtPOINT's Financial Pressure Index also flags the same building as Struggling — rates +74% vs 2023, FPI 44.38/100. The Save Our Pubs campaign's structural argument, made personal, on the bar in front of us.

item.title: Four Pubs a Day Are Closing. The Telegraph's Answer Is a Free Pint.
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Saturday 16 May 2026 is The Telegraph's National Pub Day. Renegade Brewery — the West Berkshire outfit that took on the West Berkshire estate after the 2023 collapse — has brewed Telegraph Ale, a 4% English bitter, for cask delivery to nearly 300 participating pubs. The campaign sits inside Save Our Pubs, the Telegraph's response to a closure rate of roughly four pubs a day.

item.title: From Half of British Beer to Half a Percent — What Happened to Mild
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: In 1959 mild was still half the beer Britain drank. By 2011 it was 0.5%. How Britain's everyday beer became a rarity — and why Mild May is trying to bring it back. Pattinson, Cornell and Boak & Bailey on the collapse from a 1700s young-ale category through the 1930s peak to the post-war floor; the modern fork between Black Country stalwarts at 3-3.5% and craft revisionists at 6-10%.

item.title: The Sparkler Question — A Field Guide to Britain's Quietest Cask Argument
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: A small nozzle fitted to the end of a cask tap reshapes your pint — head, mouthfeel, perceived bitterness. 140 years of British cask argument, mostly a Northern tradition with London outposts at the Queen's Arms and the Punch Tavern. From 14 May 2026, the Whippet adds Boddingtons cask with a sparkler to the Liverpool Street commute.

item.title: The Whippet: Why This Could Be One of London's Most Interesting Beer Openings of 2026
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: June update: The Whippet still is not pouring Augustiner, but it has quietly built one of the more unusual lager line-ups near Liverpool Street — Bitburger, Kutna Hora, Tegernseer, Benediktiner and more — alongside Boddingtons cask with a sparkler. The story now is not one famous Munich badge, but a craft-leaning City bar taking continental lager seriously.

item.title: Radio City Turns 7: 7 Cans, 7 Sins, 35 Launch Venues — and One Big Saturday in Chelmsford
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Saturday 9 May 2026 — Radio City Beer Works marks its seventh birthday with a brewery party in Radley Green, preceded by the nationwide can release of The 7 Deadly Sins on Thursday 7 May across 35 first-pour venues UK-wide. Brewery party logistics, full sin × brewery × style line-up, and the launch-venue locator.

item.title: Blackhorse Beer Mile 4th Birthday: All 10 Participating Venues with PINtPOINT Links
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Sunday 3 May 2026 — Walthamstow's brewery-district mile turns four with an all-day, free-entry, family-friendly takeover across 10 east-London craft venues. Every participating venue with a PINtPOINT deeplink plus the canonical Hall of Fame crawl.

item.title: Augustiner on Draft, Finally: Six Confirmed UK Pubs Pouring It
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Augustiner Helles launched on UK draught in November 2025 via James Clay & Sons with no public venue list. PINtPOINT cross-references public Untappd data to confirm six UK pubs pouring it — across London, Manchester, Newcastle, Luton and Glasgow — with a seventh, the Whippet, opening 14 May.

item.title: 12 Years of Bosko: All 12 UK Venues Pouring Pressure Drop's West Coast IPA on 1 May 2026
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Twelve years of Pressure Drop's iconic West Coast IPA, celebrated 1 May 2026 with simultaneous pours at 12 UK venues. Full launch-venue list with PINtPOINT links and back-story on Bosko.

item.title: Left Field Beer: One of Britain's Best Independent Beer Shops
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Fresh American imports (Trillium, Hop Butcher), European specialists (Arpus, CoolHead, Factory) and championed UK indies (DEYA, Buxton), curated from a single Solihull shop — with the deep-dive on why drinking American craft properly is so expensive over here.

item.title: Bass Pale Ale: The Red Triangle That Took an English Beer Global
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Before consumer brands were global, Bass already was. The red triangle of Bass Pale Ale travelled to India, Russia and America in the 19th century, riding hard Burton water, coke-malted pale grain and clear glass into a worldwide reputation. The story from 1777 through the Bass Charrington years, the Molson Coors split, and modern attempts to recover Burton pale ale heritage.

item.title: A Postcard from London: The City That Taught the World to Brew, Then Started Again
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: London is one of the oldest beer cities in the world, but the version you can drink now is barely fifteen years old. The third in the postcard trilogy — a comeback layered over an inheritance.

item.title: Walking the Ted Lasso Pub Trail in Richmond — From Crown & Anchor to Richmond Hill
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: The Crown & Anchor exterior is The Prince's Head on Richmond Green. The view above the Thames bend is the one Ted reflects from. A four-stop walking pub trail for fans of the show — and a shortlist of the wider Richmond craft-beer scene worth a pint while you're there.

item.title: A Postcard from Melbourne: The City That Built a Craft-Beer Scene on Bars, Not Breweries
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Companion to the San Diego piece — the structural opposite. Catfish, Local Taphouse, Cookie, Hippo, Boilermaker — a scene that turned curation into a category of its own.

item.title: The Drinker's Guide to Hops: What Each Variety Actually Tastes Like
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: A drinker-first reference to the 30-odd hops you're most likely to meet on a UK or European bar — Cascade, Citra, Mosaic, EKG, Saaz and the rest, organised by a six-category flavour framework.

item.title: A Postcard from San Diego: How a Navy Town Became the IPA Capital of the World
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Karl Strauss in 1989. Stone and Ballast Point in 1996. A hop arms race through the 2000s that perfected the West Coast IPA. A tour of how San Diego quietly became the IPA capital and where to drink its legacy today.

item.title: The Chelmsford Beer Mile: A Live Guide to Essex's Best Craft-Beer Crawl
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: Eight specialist venues in a mile — bottle shops, a brewpub, railway arch taprooms — walked in the right order. With live tap lists for every stop, plus a footnote on Baddow Brewery (1798–1927) and how Chelmsford Brew Co broke the century-long quiet.

item.title: What's Wrong With Beer Recommendation Systems (And What PINtPOINT Does Differently)
item.dc:creator: Sophie Ro
item.description: A response to Ethan Haley's Untappd-as-a-recommender analysis and NYC Data Science's Ninkasi project. Three walls rating-based recommenders hit — cold start, popularity bias, geography-blindness — and how PINtDEXTER layers around them.
